metadata.title: Detection, Tracking and Identification in Counter Unmanned Aircraft Systems (C-UAS)
metadata.abstract:
‑ A commonly used term in the context of counter unmanned aircraft systems (C UAS) is detection, tracking and identification (DTI). DTI includes detection, localisation, tracking, classification and identification. This report provides a comprehensive overview of detection, localisation and tracking technology, highlighting the complexities and challenges involved, and touches upon classification and identification. 

A shared context is established by defining key terminology and concepts, such as C-UAS, C‑UAS ‑ ‑ sensor, C UAS system, and C UAS solution. Sensing refers to the use of technologies and techniques ‑ for detecting physical phenomenon: various C UAS sensing modalities are explored, including acoustic, electro-optical, radar, and radio frequency (RF) sensors. Strengths and limitations of each modality are highlighted, as well as the importance of sensor data fusion in achieving more effective and robust C‑UAS detection, localisation and tracking. 

The report provides a technology overview and serves as practical guidance and operational support, for community-building and information sharing, among stakeholders involved in C‑UAS. It is aimed at readers who require a deeper understanding of the complexities and challenges involved in C‑UAS detection, tracking and identification, and who wish to stay up to date with the ‑ latest developments in C UAS technology. 

3

secondary_disciplines.level1: 电子、通信与自动控制技术

evidence.original_text: A commonly used term in the context of counter unmanned aircraft systems (C UAS) is detection, tracking and identification (DTI). DTI includes detection, localisation, tracking, classification and identification. This report provides a comprehensive overview of detection, localisation and tracking technology, highlighting the complexities and challenges involved, and touches upon classification and identification. A shared context is established by defining key terminology and concepts, such as C-UAS, C‑UAS sensor, C UAS system, and C UAS solution. Sensing refers to the use of technologies and techniques for detecting physical phenomenon: various C UAS sensing modalities are explored, including acoustic, electro-optical, radar, and radio frequency (RF) sensors. Strengths and limitations of each modality are highlighted, as well as the importance of sensor data fusion in achieving more effective and robust C‑UAS detection, localisation and tracking. The report provides a technology overview and serves as practical guidance and operational support, for community-building and information sharing, among stakeholders involved in C‑UAS. It is aimed at readers who require a deeper understanding of the complexities and challenges involved in C‑UAS detection, tracking and identification, and who wish to stay up to date with the latest developments in C UAS technology.

A commonly used term in the context of counter unmanned aircraft systems (C UAS) is detection, tracking and identification (DTI). DTI includes detection, localisation, tracking, classification and identification. This report provides a comprehensive overview of detection, localisation and tracking technology, highlighting the complexities and challenges involved, and touches upon classification and identification. A shared context is established by defining key terminology and concepts, such as C-UAS, C‑UAS sensor, C UAS system, and C UAS solution. Sensing refers to the use of technologies and techniques for detecting physical phenomenon: various C UAS sensing modalities are explored, including acoustic, electro-optical, radar, and radio frequency (RF) sensors. Strengths and limitations of each modality are highlighted, as well as the importance of sensor data fusion in achieving more effective and robust C‑UAS detection, localisation and tracking. The report provides a technology overview and serves as practical guidance and operational support, for community-building and information sharing, among stakeholders involved in C‑UAS. It is aimed at readers who require a deeper understanding of the complexities and challenges involved in C‑UAS detection, tracking and identification, and who wish to stay up to date with the latest developments in C UAS technology.

The main C‑UAS related definitions are: — Counter UAS (C UAS) Counter the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS sensor A device or system that produces an output signal for the purpose of detecting physical phenomenon relevant for C‑UAS. — C‑UAS system A system to counter the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS solution A collection of systems, stakeholders and processes for countering the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS operation A complex and coordinated effort to counter potential threats from UAS. A C‑UAS protection model provides a comprehensive framework that outlines the consecutive steps involved in C‑UAS operations, which is essential for effective C‑UAS. The next section will provide an example of a C‑UAS protection model and contains clear definitions of many relevant C‑UAS terms. These definitions are used consistently throughout this report.

The main C‑UAS related definitions are: — Counter UAS (C UAS) Counter the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS sensor A device or system that produces an output signal for the purpose of detecting physical phenomenon relevant for C‑UAS. — C‑UAS system A system to counter the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS solution A collection of systems, stakeholders and processes for countering the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS operation A complex and coordinated effort to counter potential threats from UAS. A C‑UAS protection model provides a comprehensive framework that outlines the consecutive steps involved in C‑UAS operations, which is essential for effective C‑UAS. The next section will provide an example of a C‑UAS protection model and contains clear definitions of many relevant C‑UAS terms. These definitions are used consistently throughout this report.

The main C‑UAS related definitions are: — Counter UAS (C UAS) Counter the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS sensor A device or system that produces an output signal for the purpose of detecting physical phenomenon relevant for C‑UAS. — C‑UAS system A system to counter the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS solution A collection of systems, stakeholders and processes for countering the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS operation A complex and coordinated effort to counter potential threats from UAS. A C‑UAS protection model provides a comprehensive framework that outlines the consecutive steps involved in C‑UAS operations, which is essential for effective C‑UAS. The next section will provide an example of a C‑UAS protection model and contains clear definitions of many relevant C‑UAS terms. These definitions are used consistently throughout this report.

Elements of the cyclic C UAS protection model are: — Preparation and prevention C‑UAS operations start with preparation, defined as initiating activities for the implementation and/or improvement of a C UAS solution. Examples are setting up a C UAS solution (Hansen, P., Pinto Faria, R., 2023), allocating the human resources to operate the solution, making sure everybody is properly trained and verifying the proper functioning of the C‑UAS solution. It is advisable to execute prevention activities for countering the potential threats from UAS in parallel. Prevention can involve physical hardening of buildings and sites, as described in the Handbook on UAS Risk Assessment and Principles for Physical Hardening of Buildings and Sites (Karlos, V. and Larcher, M., 2023). Other examples of prevention are organizing a media campaign and putting up signs for informing the public that it is not allowed to operate UAS within a specific area. — Detection Once a C‑UAS solution has been put in place, detection activities can start. Detection is defined as a declaration that a UAS is in the presence of a C UAS device or system. Detection can be done by various types of systems that make use of different emissions that UAS generate.

— Tracking Once a UAS has been detected, the next step is to establish the location of a UAS or UAS related element, something that is referred to as localisation. The process of generating a compilation of the locations of the UAS over time is referred to as tracking.

— Tracking Once a UAS has been detected, the next step is to establish the location of a UAS or UAS related element, something that is referred to as localisation. The process of generating a compilation of the locations of the UAS over time is referred to as tracking.

— Identification Identification may consist of both classification and identification. Classification is defined as assigning the UAS to a category. An example of a high-level classification is that a UAS is categorized as a fixed wing or as a multi-rotor. A more detailed type of classification could be based on manufacturer, make and model of a UAS.

— Identification Identification may consist of both classification and identification. Classification is defined as assigning the UAS to a category. An example of a high-level classification is that a UAS is categorized as a fixed wing or as a multi-rotor. A more detailed type of classification could be based on manufacturer, make and model of a UAS. Identification is defined as assigning a unique identifier to a UAS or UAS component, such as the UA serial number and the operator registration. An example of identification information is the information that is broadcasted by a direct remote ID (DRI) system on board of the UAS.

Acoustic UAS sensing systems often consist of one or more digital acoustic sensors (microphones) for registering acoustic signals (sound), with the resulting data then being processed by a signal processing system. Commercially available systems focus on passive detection, i.e. detecting the sound that the UAS generates. Active systems send out acoustic signals and listen to the reflections (echoes), like echolocation as used by flying bats. For detecting UAS, the signal processing system of the acoustic sensing system tries to recognize the acoustic signal characteristics of UA in the collection of registered acoustic signals. For doing so, it analyses the received acoustic signals: when e.g. spectral, timbral and/or temporal features of an acoustic signal match with that of a (known) UA, the system will indicate the presence of a UA. When the UA sound is sufficiently distinctly recognised as belonging to a specific type of UA, the system could also report the specific type. The acoustic detection process is illustrated in Figure 14.

Digital cameras, also referred to as electro optical systems, translate light into electronic data. As illustrated in the diagram of the electromagnetic spectrum in section 2.2, there are many different types of electromagnetic radiation that are referred to as light. There is visible light, infrared (IR) and ultraviolet (UV). Each type of light has its own type of electrical optical sensing system. An example of a combined visual range and IR camera is provided in Figure 18. The sun is a natural emitter of a broad spectrum of electromagnetic radiation that includes infrared, visible light and ultraviolet. Depending on various factors, such as the material and colour of the UA, these types of radiation are reflected by a UA. UAS often also emit light, e.g. if a UA is equipped with navigation lights. As all objects with a temperature above the absolute zero emit infrared light, this also holds for UAS. And when a UA is equipped with a rocket or jet engine, when engaged, the engine will also emit ultraviolet (UV) light. Cameras often consist of an (adaptable) lens and a filter that aims to filter out anything but the desired type of light, and projects it on a sensor that then translates the received infrared light into electronic data. This electronic data can then be transported via electronic networks and be used to create a visual representation of the image captured by the camera, e.g. on a monitor. The signal can also be used for further processing, e.g. for automated detection and tracking purposes. More general information on electro optical (EO) sensing systems can be found in Annex 5.

The term RADAR is an acronym that stands for “RAdio Detection And Ranging”. Both active and passive radar-based sensing systems exist. Active radar systems consist of an emitter that sends out radio frequency waves and one or more antenna(s) to receive reflected radio frequency waves. Based on the reflected waves, the radar detects, localises and tracks objects. Passive radar systems make use of radio frequency waves already present in the atmosphere to detect, localise and track objects. Both active and passive radar-based sensing systems use signal processing of the received radio frequency waves to create an image of their surroundings. Active radar systems emit radio frequency waves that travel through the atmosphere, and if these radio frequency waves hit an object (e.g. a bird, rain drops, UAS, civil airliners, …) they are reflected and scattered in various directions, the latter often including the direction of the radar system. A simplified example of this is depicted in Figure 21. The more reflective (related to the signal frequency) or the bigger the object is, the larger the energy of radio frequency waves that is returned. The returning radio frequency waves, like terminology used in sound, is referred to as an (radar) echo.

Radio frequency (RF) waves are often used for transmitting information between a UA and the remote control (RC) of the UAS. The steering commands of the pilot to control the UA are an example of information sent from a remote control (RC) to a UA. The video feed of an on‑board camera is an example of information being sent from the UA to the RC or a video link device. For regular commercial UAS, the frequencies used for transmitting the information between UA and RC lie within the so‑called ISM bands, parts of the radio spectrum that are reserved for industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) purposes. Pictures of several RF based systems are shown in Figure 25. RF sensing systems detect the presence of UAS by monitoring RF signals on the radio frequencies often used by UAS and their remote control (RC) means. Some systems therefore scan the common frequency bands used for UAS related communication. Based on a library of UA and RC signals, these systems try to recognize if UAS and RCs are actively communicating with each other. The performance of such systems, amongst others, relies heavily upon the processing power of the system, the frequency ranges covered, the overall bandwidth covered, and the collection of known signals being scanned for. Some systems may limit themselves to only the specific frequencies used by specific brand of UAS, to improve the performance of the system specifically for that type of UAS. When the characteristics of a received signals match with that of a (known) UA, RC and/or other related RF device, the detection system assumes the presence of a UAS.

In many C‑UAS operations the human element will also provide a sensing capacity, as C‑UAS operations often also include dedicated UAS spotters. The UA spotters can fill in blind spots of the C‑UAS system, and cover for when the C‑UAS system has a technical problem. Next to that, external persons on the street, e.g. police officers or security personnel, might detect, localise and/or track a UAS and report it to the C‑UAS solution. Human sensing goes back a long time, see e.g. Figure 27, and depends on many aspects. These include factors related to the observer, characteristics of the UAS, as the setting is also relevant and additional conditions, e.g. the weather, the time of day, etc.

Sensor data fusion receives data/information from multiple sensing systems, and aims to consolidate the data into more accurate, reliable and comprehensive information than what the sensing systems can provide individually., as illustrated in Figure 32. The data fusion process is far from straightforward and introduces many challenges. One of the challenging aspects is that the data from the different sensing systems is likely to be based on different moments in time, arrive at different times, with different update frequencies and different uncertainties. The sensor data fusion algorithm has to figure out how to piece all the data together into an accurate, reliable, and comprehensive overview.

A commonly used term in the context of counter unmanned aircraft systems (C UAS) is detection, tracking and identification (DTI). DTI includes detection, localisation, tracking, classification and identification. This report provides a comprehensive overview of detection, localisation and tracking technology, highlighting the complexities and challenges involved, and touches upon classification and identification. A shared context is established by defining key terminology and concepts, such as C-UAS, C‑UAS sensor, C UAS system, and C UAS solution. Sensing refers to the use of technologies and techniques for detecting physical phenomenon: various C UAS sensing modalities are explored, including acoustic, electro-optical, radar, and radio frequency (RF) sensors. Strengths and limitations of each modality are highlighted, as well as the importance of sensor data fusion in achieving more effective and robust C‑UAS detection, localisation and tracking. The report provides a technology overview and serves as practical guidance and operational support, for community-building and information sharing, among stakeholders involved in C‑UAS. It is aimed at readers who require a deeper understanding of the complexities and challenges involved in C‑UAS detection, tracking and identification, and who wish to stay up to date with the latest developments in C UAS technology.

Counter unmanned aircraft systems (C UAS) solutions are designed to counter the potential threat from unmanned aircraft systems (UAS). A C UAS solution integrates a broad range of interacting techniques. This report provides an introduction into some of these techniques and implementing technologies for C UAS detection, tracking, and identification. The intention is to provide the reader with: - clear definitions of detection, tracking and identification; - generic insight in the basic principles behind detection, localisation and tracking; - an overview of the different technologies available, with for each technology: - insight in the basic working and the most relevant aspects; - strengths and limitations of the technology. - basic insight in how the individual technologies can be combined into: - a design for a C‑UAS solution; - consolidated findings of the system as a whole (fusion). The optimal usage of C UAS solutions requires thorough knowledge of its underlying technologies. After reading the report, the reader should have obtained a basic understanding of the most relevant aspects in C UAS detection, tracking and identification.

The main C‑UAS related definitions are: — Counter UAS (C UAS) Counter the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS sensor A device or system that produces an output signal for the purpose of detecting physical phenomenon relevant for C‑UAS. — C‑UAS system A system to counter the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS solution A collection of systems, stakeholders and processes for countering the potential threats from UAS. — C‑UAS operation A complex and coordinated effort to counter potential threats from UAS. A C‑UAS protection model provides a comprehensive framework that outlines the consecutive steps involved in C‑UAS operations, which is essential for effective C‑UAS. The next section will provide an example of a C‑UAS protection model and contains clear definitions of many relevant C‑UAS terms. These definitions are used consistently throughout this report.

evidence.section: 2.1 Simple example of human UAS detection and tracking
evidence.original_text: Table 1 provides an overview of example factors that influence the performance of a human spotter. The table also provides an example indication of a lower bound (relatively easy situations) and an upper bound (relatively difficult situations) for these factors influencing the performance of a human spotter. Table 1 illustrates several factors that contribute to the performance of a spotter. Some have to do with the spotter, e.g. when spotting is the only task at hand, it is easier to spot a UA compared to when other tasks also must be attended. Some of the factors can be compensated for, often only to a certain extent, as e.g. the spotter might be able to compensate for nighttime darkness by using a flashlight to illuminate the UA. But many other factors cannot be influenced by the spotter. Figure 6 illustrates several of the many performance-influencing factors: A bigger UA (1) is easier to spot than a smaller one (2). A close by UA (1) is easier to spot than a faraway UA (3) A UA in dry weather (1) is easier to spot than a UA in heavy rain conditions (4) A UA in clear sight (1) is easier to spot than one (partially) behind a building (5). Annex 2 contains a more extensive example overview of performance influencing factors for a C‑UAS solution.

entries.name: Human spotter performance influencing factors dataset

evidence.original_text: Although it is clearly difficult to determine a maximal range for detectability by a human observer, Table 5 provides notional maximal detection ranges for “representative” types of UAS. Notional maximal visual detection ranges* * Based on a literature study, assessments and experience, for “representative” types of UAS under “normal” conditions. source: Royal NLR, EUROCONTROL High-level Workshop (Poppinga)

evidence.section: 3.6 Summary of detection, localisation and tracking techniques
evidence.original_text: This chapter has provided details on how the different types of sensing techniques function. The most important characteristics for the various detection, localisation and tracking techniques have been touched upon: these have been consolidated in Table 6. The table cells have been colour‑coded to facilitate easier understanding of its contents: red means negative, orange means neutral, green means positive and yellow is an indication of comments. The next chapter discusses combining the different sensing techniques. Table 6. Summarizing generalised indicative overview of several main characteristics for the various detection, localisation and tracking modalities.

evidence.original_text: Different definitions have come into existence for the terminology used within the UAS and the C‑UAS community. This report follows Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/945 (European Commission, 2019) and (EU) 2019/947 (European Commission, 2019a), which define a UAS as an unmanned aircraft system, consisting of an unmanned aircraft (UA) and the elements to operate it. A UA is any aircraft operating or designed to operate autonomously or to be piloted remotely without a pilot on board. Figure 2 illustrates elements of a UAS, together with support equipment and the human element. An overview of various UAS and C‑UAS related definitions can be found in Annex 1. The definitions used within this report are also aligned with those of the Communication from the Commission on countering potential threats posed by drones, COM (2023) 659 final (European Commission, 2023) and the Handbook on UAS protection of Critical Infrastructure and Public Space: A five Phase approach for C‑UAS stakeholders (Hansen, P., Pinto Faria, R., 2023). The main C‑UAS related definitions are: Counter UAS (C UAS) Counter the potential threats from UAS. C‑UAS sensor A device or system that produces an output signal for the purpose of detecting physical phenomenon relevant for C‑UAS. C‑UAS system A system to counter the potential threats from UAS. C‑UAS solution A collection of systems, stakeholders and processes for countering the potential threats from UAS. C‑UAS operation A complex and coordinated effort to counter potential threats from UAS.

entries.description: Standardized definitions for C-UAS detection, tracking and identification terminology

evidence.original_text: A commonly used term in the context of C UAS is detection, tracking and identification (DTI). DTI includes detection, localisation, tracking, classification and identification, which are discussed in more detail below. UAS detection is the declaration that a UAS is in the presence of a C‑UAS sensor. A detection can relate to the UA as the flying element of a UAS, but also to other UAS elements, such as a remote control (RC) or a video link receiver. Once a (suspected) UAS presence has been detected, the next step is to establish the location of the UAS and/or UAS related element(s) – this is referred to a localisation. Once an element has been localised, its movements should be monitored and stored in a compilation of locations over time, which is referred to as tracking. In parallel, immediately after detection, the activities for classification and/or identification can start. When a UAS has been detected by a C UAS system, a confirmation of the detection is strongly advisable. The purpose of confirmation is to verify if the detection truly relates to a UAS and not something else, like a bird or a spoofed UAS, the latter being e.g. the deliberate simulation of UAS radio signals without a UAS being present. The earlier a confirmation step takes place, the more efficient and effective the use of the C‑UAS resources will be. The different steps of detection, localisation, tracking, classification and identification, together with a suggested confirmation activity, are shown in Figure 4.

entries.description: Operational definitions for detection, localisation, tracking, classification and identification within C-UAS DTI

evidence.original_text: Some sensors require line‑of‑sight (LoS) to be able to detect, localise and track UAS or UAS-related elements. LoS refers to the absence of obstructions between the sensor and the UAS or UAS-related element. The implication of LoS is that objects that block (parts of) the sensor coverage shape create blind spots for the sensor, so called occlusion areas. Figure 11 provides an example of the coverage of a sensor being limited by an obstacle. In areas with many objects, like urban environments, obstacles create many challenges when trying to optimize coverage for a C UAS system set-up. The 3D coverage map of LoS based sensors is relatively easy to determine, as it is relatively easy to determine their blind spots. Blind spots can be reduced or eliminated by adding sensors that (partially) cover the blind spot. The coverage area for some sensors is not necessarily determined by line‑of‑sight. These types of sensors function based on the propagation of emissions. They merely focus on the presence of an emission, without the need to receive the emission directly from the UAS. Radio frequency based sensors, for example, allow for emissions to be reflected and refracted by all sorts of objects before they reach the sensor, as long as the emission levels are still sufficiently clearly distinguishable from background noise. The propagation-based type of sensor allows for coverage of areas that not necessarily have line‑of‑sight and might allow for “looking around a corner”. Figure 12 provides an example of the propagation of RF signals not being limited to line of sight. Propagation-based sensors have a coverage area that is hard to determine, making it difficult to identify blind spots. Calculating the coverage area is complex, as it requires sophisticated propagation calculations that make use of detailed 3D models, including information on the materials at the building façade and their reflectiveness for the specific type of emissions. As an alternative to calculations, the coverage area can be sampled by running coverage tests at a 3D grid of test points.

entries.description: Line-of-sight and propagation requirements for different C-UAS sensing modalities

evidence.original_text: The concept of false negatives and false positives is very important for C UAS in general. These are defined as indicated in Table 3: green text cells indicate what is true, the red text cells indicate what is not true (false). Table 3. Reality versus perception, illustration of true/false positives and true/false negatives. The rate of false positives and false negatives is highly relevant for the overall performance of a C‑UAS solution. From an operational perspective, false positives could lead to significantly reduced performance of a C UAS solution: after a series of false positives, the human element (e.g. the C‑UAS operator or follow-up officers on the street) might decide that an alert by the system does not have much value. False negatives only become obvious if through other means it becomes apparent that there is/was a UAS present, while it was not detected by the C UAS system. This could also significantly reduce trust that the human element has in the C‑UAS solution. There are many situations that could cause false positives and negatives, varying from an improperly functioning system, connectivity problems, incorrect data processing and human error. False positives are relatively easy to check for, as they merely require a reliable confirmation by a second means. A regular method for verification is through a human C UAS system operator that uses a pan-tilt-zoom camera for a visual confirmation. False negatives are harder to address than false positives, as noting their occurrence requires knowledge of something that is mostly not known. Defining a structured process to learn from situations in which a UAS was not detected where it should have been, provides a way to address false negatives. Such an improvement process might however cost significant throughput time and will most likely need to be coupled to a research and innovation (R&I) process of the manufacturer(s) of the system.

entries.description: False positive and false negative definitions and operational implications for C-UAS system performance

evidence.original_text: Classification and identification are closely related to each other. Classification is defined as assigning a UAS to a category, e.g. by type, make and model. Identification is defined as assigning a unique identifier to a UAS or UAS component, such as the UA serial number and the operator registration. Whereas classification allows for different UAS to be assigned in the same category, identification for each UAS should be unique. Classification and identification aim to consolidate sensor data into easy-to-understand information that is highly valuable for the consecutive decision-making process. Identification and classification are sometimes used interchangeably by different audiences. Classification is dependent upon the different categories defined. An example of a high-level type of categorization is to distinguish between a fixed wing and a multi-rotor. A more detailed type of classification could be on manufacturer, make and model of a UAS, e.g. classifying a UAS as a DJI Mavic 2 Pro multirotor. The categories that are in use can differ per C UAS system. Based on the (set of) features captured by the C UAS sensors, classification assigns potentially a detection or a track to a category. The different features captured should be sufficiently discriminative to allow for the required classification. Whereas the radar reflections of a UA might be sufficient to classify it either as a fixed wing or a multi-rotor, categorization of a UA in manufacturer, make and model might require visual range images and RF signature information of the UA. Identification focusses on assigning a unique identifier to a UAS or UAS component, either based on features captured by C‑UAS sensors, or as manually added. Data captured by a C‑UAS sensor used for identification could comprise of a single type of feature, like physical or MAC address or information that is broadcasted by a direct remote ID (DRI) system on board of the UAS. As some features can be spoofed, it could be risky to rely on a single type of signature for identification only, Identification that uses a combination of different types of features that jointly are sufficiently unique, e.g. combing DRI with visual information and an RF signature, could provide increased assurance of the correctness of the identification.

entries.description: Classification and identification definitions and data requirements for C-UAS systems

evidence.original_text: Acoustic UAS sensing systems often consist of one or more digital acoustic sensors (microphones) for registering acoustic signals (sound), with the resulting data then being processed by a signal processing system. Commercially available systems focus on passive detection, i.e. detecting the sound that the UAS generates. Active systems send out acoustic signals and listen to the reflections (echoes), like echolocation as used by flying bats. For detecting UAS, the signal processing system of the acoustic sensing system tries to recognize the acoustic signal characteristics of UA in the collection of registered acoustic signals. For doing so, it analyses the received acoustic signals: when e.g. spectral, timbral and/or temporal features of an acoustic signal match with that of a (known) UA, the system will indicate the presence of a UA. When the UA sound is sufficiently distinctly recognised as belonging to a specific type of UA, the system could also report the specific type. The acoustic detection process is illustrated in Figure 14. The level of a sound, the sound pressure level, is expressed in decibel (dB). With respect to using acoustic sensing for detection and tracking, the ambient (background) sound level is of importance. In general, when a sound level is below the ambient sound level, it is difficult to notice. Sound levels decrease rapidly over distance, as a simplified model of the sound level as a function of distance shows in Figure 15. When an acoustic sensing system is equipped with multiple acoustic sensors located at geographically different locations, it might be possible to localize the origin of an acoustic signal. Techniques for determining the direction/location of an emitter are described in Annex 4. If the microphones are organised in an array, as e.g. illustrated in Figure 16, it allows not only to localize sound sources, but also to use beamforming to focus on receiving emissions from a specific direction and cancelling out other directions. This could increase the range of an acoustic sensing system.

Digital cameras, also referred to as electro optical systems, translate light into electronic data. As illustrated in the diagram of the electromagnetic spectrum in section 2.2, there are many different types of electromagnetic radiation that are referred to as light. There is visible light, infrared (IR) and ultraviolet (UV). Each type of light has its own type of electrical optical sensing system. An example of a combined visual range and IR camera is provided in Figure 18. The sun is a natural emitter of a broad spectrum of electromagnetic radiation that includes infrared, visible light and ultraviolet. Depending on various factors, such as the material and colour of the UA, these types of radiation are reflected by a UA. UAS often also emit light, e.g. if a UA is equipped with navigation lights. As all objects with a temperature above the absolute zero emit infrared light, this also holds for UAS. And when a UA is equipped with a rocket or jet engine, when engaged, the engine will also emit ultraviolet (UV) light. Cameras often consist of an (adaptable) lens and a filter that aims to filter out anything but the desired type of light, and projects it on a sensor that then translates the received infrared light into electronic data. This electronic data can then be transported via electronic networks and be used to create a visual representation of the image captured by the camera, e.g. on a monitor. The signal can also be used for further processing, e.g. for automated detection and tracking purposes. More general information on electro optical (EO) sensing systems can be found in Annex 5. Different types of cameras focus on the use of different types of light: infrared range cameras focus on the infrared range, visual range camera’s focus on the visual range, ultraviolet on the UV range, and multispectral focus on a broad range of light. Sometimes different types of cameras are combined into a single system, which allows the user to switch between the images in different light spectra. The different types of cameras are discussed in more detail below.

The term RADAR is an acronym that stands for “RAdio Detection And Ranging”. Both active and passive radar-based sensing systems exist. Active radar systems consist of an emitter that sends out radio frequency waves and one or more antenna(s) to receive reflected radio frequency waves. Based on the reflected waves, the radar detects, localises and tracks objects. Passive radar systems make use of radio frequency waves already present in the atmosphere to detect, localise and track objects. Both active and passive radar-based sensing systems use signal processing of the received radio frequency waves to create an image of their surroundings. Active radar systems emit radio frequency waves that travel through the atmosphere, and if these radio frequency waves hit an object (e.g. a bird, rain drops, UAS, civil airliners, …) they are reflected and scattered in various directions, the latter often including the direction of the radar system. A simplified example of this is depicted in Figure 21. The more reflective (related to the signal frequency) or the bigger the object is, the larger the energy of radio frequency waves that is returned. The returning radio frequency waves, like terminology used in sound, is referred to as an (radar) echo. Processing of the returned radio frequency waves, depending on the functionality of the radar system as a whole, allows it to determine one or more aspects of the direction (azimuth/bearing), distance (also known as range) and elevation of the object(s) that caused the echo. Figure 23 illustates the most relevant localisation related terminology for a radar system. Based on the underlying technology, different types of radar can be distinguished. If the radar transmitter and receiver are collocated (located at the same place) on a static position, this is being referred to as mono-static. Multi-static radar systems make use of a single transmitter and multiple receivers that are placed at different locations. As a result, they have enhanced detection capabilities compared to mono-static. Based on the differences in the receipt of an echo at the different receivers of a multi-static radar system, a central system calculates the locations of detected objects. Multi-static radar systems are more accurate than mono-static radar systems, at the cost of higher complexity. Passive radar systems do not emit radio frequency waves at all, in contrast to active radar systems, but make use of radio frequency waves already present in the atmosphere for other purposes, such as digital television broadcasts and communication signals. Passive radar systems measure the time difference in the arrival of radio frequency waves that arrive directly from the transmitter and those arriving from the reflection of an object, to determine the location of an object and track the object over time.

Radio frequency (RF) waves are often used for transmitting information between a UA and the remote control (RC) of the UAS. The steering commands of the pilot to control the UA are an example of information sent from a remote control (RC) to a UA. The video feed of an on‑board camera is an example of information being sent from the UA to the RC or a video link device. For regular commercial UAS, the frequencies used for transmitting the information between UA and RC lie within the so‑called ISM bands, parts of the radio spectrum that are reserved for industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) purposes. Pictures of several RF based systems are shown in Figure 25. RF sensing systems detect the presence of UAS by monitoring RF signals on the radio frequencies often used by UAS and their remote control (RC) means. Some systems therefore scan the common frequency bands used for UAS related communication. Based on a library of UA and RC signals, these systems try to recognize if UAS and RCs are actively communicating with each other. The performance of such systems, amongst others, relies heavily upon the processing power of the system, the frequency ranges covered, the overall bandwidth covered, and the collection of known signals being scanned for. Some systems may limit themselves to only the specific frequencies used by specific brand of UAS, to improve the performance of the system specifically for that type of UAS. When the characteristics of a received signals match with that of a (known) UA, RC and/or other related RF device, the detection system assumes the presence of a UAS. Next to being provided with a notification of the presence of a UAS, most situations require information about the location of the detected emitter. The location of the source of the signal can be determined in various ways. If the protocol used is known, and the data contained within the signal is either not encrypted or can be decrypted, the exchanged information can be inspected. When the UA and the RC exchange information on their GNSS location(s), this information can then be used to retrieve the location of UA and RC. However, the information is often exchanged using proprietary protocols and/or encryption. As a result, detailed knowledge of the protocol and/or encryption is often a prerequisite for this to be able to work. As a reference, the OSI model (OSI, 2024) can be used for establishing at which level in the protocol the information is extracted, as is illustrated in Annex 3. Alternatively, if the information exchanged between the UA and the RC is not accessible, signal source localisation techniques like time of arrival (ToA) or angle of arrival (AoA), as described in Annex 4, might be able to localize the origin of the signals. This however requires that the RF based sensing is done from multiple locations.

In many C‑UAS operations the human element will also provide a sensing capacity, as C‑UAS operations often also include dedicated UAS spotters. The UA spotters can fill in blind spots of the C‑UAS system, and cover for when the C‑UAS system has a technical problem. Next to that, external persons on the street, e.g. police officers or security personnel, might detect, localise and/or track a UAS and report it to the C‑UAS solution. Human sensing goes back a long time, see e.g. Figure 27, and depends on many aspects. These include factors related to the observer, characteristics of the UAS, as the setting is also relevant and additional conditions, e.g. the weather, the time of day, etc. This section briefly touches upon some relevant aspects of human sensing. For the human ear, the just notable difference (JND) in sound levels is quite relevant for detection, localisation and tracking. The JND is typically considered to be 3-5 dB, meaning that the sound of a UA needs to be at least 3-5 dB higher than the ambient (background) sound level, for a person to be likely to hear the UA. Indications of the ambient sound level are 30-40 dB for a quiet area, 4050 dB for a suburban environment and 60-70 dB for a busy city. The coverage shape for individual human hearing is roughly omni-directional. In general, sound levels decrease rapidly over distance. For a relatively common small and silent UA like a DJI Mavic or Phantom, the sound level at 1 m distance is between 74-78 dB. As the sound level is reduced with 6 dB if this distance is doubled, which would mean that the sound level would be 62-66 dB if the UA would pass at 4 m distance. For a busy city with an ambient sound level of 68 dB, a distance of 4 m could already result in the UA not being noticeable for the human acoustic senses. In a quiet area, the distance could increase up to more than 100 m before the difference between the sound level of the UA and that of the environment would fall below the JND. The performance of visual detection depends on many different factors, as pointed out in section 2.1. However, regardless of the factors, the techniques used for spotting is also relevant. Some examples of different search technique are illustrated in Figure 28. Although it is clearly difficult to determine a maximal range for detectability by a human observer, Table 5 provides notional maximal detection ranges for “representative” types of UAS.

An intuitive introduction about C‑UAS detection and tracking is provided by looking at the way a ‑ human performs these tasks. Sensing, in the context of C UAS, refers to the use of technologies and ‑ ‑ techniques for detecting physical phenomena relevant for C UAS. Different types of C UAS sensing, so called C‑UAS sensing modalities, mostly operate along similar principles as the human senses do. This subsection provides an everyday example to illustrate that UAS detection, localisation and tracking performance is dependent on many factors. It shows basic principles in a manner that the ‑ reader should be able to relate to, without requiring specific prior knowledge of C UAS systems, technology and/or operations. The starting point of the example is formed by a spotter, a person that is positioned in an outdoor setting, tasked to scan the sky for UA. The spotter has normal eyesight and hearing, has a pair of binoculars to take a closer look, and is equipped with a flashlight. The setting is depicted in Figure 5. For a situation as depicted in Figure 5, it is assumed that while the spotter is active, a UA starts to manoeuvre at the verge of the detection range of the spotter. At a certain moment, the spotter notices the UA as a vague flying object. From the viewpoint of the spotter the object could be a UA, but could also be something else, like a bird or a small balloon. The spotter decides to use the pair of binoculars, zooms in on the object and concludes it is indeed a UA. As the spotter knows the area, the location of the UA is now also clear to the spotter. While the UA is manoeuvring around, the spotter tracks it by keeping the pair of binoculars focussed on the UA. The spotter has now detected a flying object, confirmed it is a UA, localised it and is actively tracking it. Many factors of different origins can influence the performance of the spotter in the above example. These spotter-performance influencing factors can be grouped in different categories, such as UA related factors, environmental factors and spotter related factors. Table 1 provides an overview of example factors that influence the performance of a human spotter. The table also provides an example indication of a lower bound (relatively easy situations) and an upper bound (relatively difficult situations) for these factors influencing the performance of a human spotter.

entries.setup: spotter equipped with binoculars and flashlight, outdoor setting, scanning sky for UA

evidence.original_text: To illustrate some of the challenges for the data fusion, a C‑UAS solution equipped with a radar, an RF sensing system and a combined visual light / IR camera, set-up around a large outdoor event, is used as an example. The radar and the RF sensing system are located close to each other, yet have different coverage areas. The coverage area of the radar is about 2 km for a small consumer UA, yet that of the RF sensing system is close to 3 km for the same type of UA. The camera is located close to both other sensing systems. Both the radar and the camera cannot see through/behind obstacles, yet due to multipath the RF sensing system’s coverage also includes some of the occluded areas. Blind C‑UAS tests are performed on the day before the event starts: unknown to the C‑UAS solution, ‑ different UA take off from various locations, to verify if the C UAS solution functions properly. ‑ During the test, the C UAS system appears to work fine: either the RF sensing system or both the RF and radar detect the UA within less 30 seconds after take-off of a UA and provide updates on the location within maximally 10 seconds in between. When both systems detect the UA, even though there are inherent uncertainties in their localisation of the UA, the sensor data fusion succeeds in matching the detections and provide a clear location of a single UA. The visual light / IR camera is used to confirm the detection and localisation of the UA. When the event starts, people start coming into the event area, and start to make use of the on-site Wi-Fi and mobile phone networks. As the frequency bands used for controlling UAS are the same as ‑ those used for Wi-Fi, the traffic on frequencies that are monitored by the C UAS RF sensing system increases significantly. This puts more and more load on the RF sensing system that tries to recognize all sorts of UAS related signals within the captured data. Due to interference the detection range for the RF sensing system starts to reduce. Then, in the evening of the event, after sundown, a UAS light show has been planned by the organisation. When the UAS of the light show start to take off, quite some people of the audience ‑ take out their mobile phone and start streaming. The C UAS system is expected to start detecting, localising and tracking the UAS of the light show shortly after take-off, yet the system turns out to require several minutes before reporting only some of the UAS, and provides infrequent updates of their locations, with sometimes more than a minute in between in which a UA potentially has travelled quite some distance. When looking into the data provided by the individual sensing systems, it turns out that the RF sensing system has a hard time detecting, localising and tracking the UAS due to the significant use of the frequency bands that it operates on. At the same time, the radar has difficulties in discriminating one UAS from another, and sometimes cannot see a UA, e.g. because another UA is flying in front of it. As a result, the data fusion algorithms get incomplete information from sometimes moments apart that are very difficult to fuse.

entries.setup: C-UAS solution equipped with radar, RF sensing system, and combined visual/IR camera, outdoor event area

evidence.original_text: For a relatively common small and silent UA like a DJI Mavic or Phantom, the sound level at 1 m distance is between 74-78 dB. As the sound level is reduced with 6 dB if this distance is doubled, which would mean that the sound level would be 62-66 dB if the UA would pass at 4 m distance. For a busy city with an ambient sound level of 68 dB, a distance of 4 m could already result in the UA not being noticeable for the human acoustic senses. In a quiet area, the distance could increase up to more than 100 m before the difference between the sound level of the UA and that of the environment would fall below the JND.

entries.context: DJI Mavic or Phantom at 1 m distance

evidence.original_text: Indications of the ambient sound level are 30-40 dB for a quiet area, 40-50 dB for a suburban environment and 60-70 dB for a busy city. The coverage shape for individual human hearing is roughly omni-directional. In general, sound levels decrease rapidly over distance.

Indications of the ambient sound level are 30-40 dB for a quiet area, 40-50 dB for a suburban environment and 60-70 dB for a busy city. The coverage shape for individual human hearing is roughly omni-directional. In general, sound levels decrease rapidly over distance.

For the human ear, the just notable difference (JND) in sound levels is quite relevant for detection, localisation and tracking. The JND is typically considered to be 3-5 dB, meaning that the sound of a UA needs to be at least 3-5 dB higher than the ambient (background) sound level, for a person to be likely to hear the UA.

For a relatively common small and silent UA like a DJI Mavic or Phantom, the sound level at 1 m distance is between 74-78 dB. As the sound level is reduced with 6 dB if this distance is doubled, which would mean that the sound level would be 62-66 dB if the UA would pass at 4 m distance.

For a relatively common small and silent UA like a DJI Mavic or Phantom, the sound level at 1 m distance is between 74-78 dB. As the sound level is reduced with 6 dB if this distance is doubled, which would mean that the sound level would be 62-66 dB if the UA would pass at 4 m distance. For a busy city with an ambient sound level of 68 dB, a distance of 4 m could already result in the UA not being noticeable for the human acoustic senses. In a quiet area, the distance could increase up to more than 100 m before the difference between the sound level of the UA and that of the environment would fall below the JND.

evidence.section: 3.6 Summary of detection, localisation and tracking techniques
evidence.original_text: The range of acoustic sensing systems can extend to hundreds of meters and depends strongly on the sound level of the UA and the ambient sound level. The range is also influenced by atmospheric conditions, e.g. heavy precipitation or strong winds. The range of radar-based sensing systems is often in the order of several kilometres. The range is strongly determined by the radar cross section (RCS) of the UA, i.e. the observable size of the radar reflective area, and the energy of the RF waves. The range can be reduced by RF propagation degrading conditions, like heavy precipitation. The range of RF based sensing systems often in the order of several kilometres to tens of kilometres, and strongly influenced by the signal strength of the transmitting UAS elements and the background sound level on the frequencies used. The range can be affected by RF propagation degrading conditions, like heavy precipitation. The range of electro optical based sensing systems is often up to one or more kilometres and is strongly influenced by the UAS element characteristics, together with the optical zoom factor and resolution of the camera. For the specific type of camera, next to the applicable (ambient) light levels, examples for characteristics of the UA are: for infrared (IR) cameras these are temperatures related; for visual light these are visual characteristics related; for UV these are related to the combustion process from its jet or rocket propulsion system. The range can be reduced by degraded light conditions, such as those resulting from twilight or nighttime, or weather conditions such as heavy precipitation and fog.

entries.compared_to: acoustic sensing vs radar vs RF sensing vs electro optical

evidence.section: 3.6 Summary of detection, localisation and tracking techniques
evidence.original_text: Radar functionality is, in general, often limited to line‑of‑sight only for the UA while airborne. The RF based sensing coverage is not limited to line‑of‑sight, as signals can also reflect several times before reaching the sensing system: in those cases, the direction from which the signal arrives does not allow for localising the source.

A commonly used term in the context of counter unmanned aircraft systems (C UAS) is detection, tracking and identification (DTI). DTI includes detection, localisation, tracking, classification and identification. This report provides a comprehensive overview of detection, localisation and tracking technology, highlighting the complexities and challenges involved, and touches upon classification and identification. A shared context is established by defining key terminology and concepts, such as C-UAS, C‑UAS sensor, C UAS system, and C UAS solution. Sensing refers to the use of technologies and techniques for detecting physical phenomenon: various C UAS sensing modalities are explored, including acoustic, electro-optical, radar, and radio frequency (RF) sensors. Strengths and limitations of each modality are highlighted, as well as the importance of sensor data fusion in achieving more effective and robust C‑UAS detection, localisation and tracking. The report provides a technology overview and serves as practical guidance and operational support, for community-building and information sharing, among stakeholders involved in C‑UAS. It is aimed at readers who require a deeper understanding of the complexities and challenges involved in C‑UAS detection, tracking and identification, and who wish to stay up to date with the latest developments in C UAS technology.

Various sensing modalities, such as acoustic, electro-optical, radar, and radio frequency (RF), are used for detection and tracking. A key aspect in understanding detection and tracking lies in understanding emissions. Different sorts of emissions play a role: Emissions in the sense of the release of energy from a source, like electromagnetic (EM) and mechanical emissions. Examples of EM emissions are radio frequency signals used to remotely control a UAS or the heat radiation of a UA. Examples of mechanical emissions are sounds from the propellers and turbulent air flows created by propellers. Emissions in the sense of the release of matter or substance, such as chemical emissions. When equipped with a piston, rocket, or jet engine, a UA produces emission gasses that might provide a way to detect if a UA is present. This type of emissions is generally not used for C UAS detection and tracking purposes, and therefore not further addressed within this report. C‑UAS sensing modalities focus on electromagnetic (EM) and mechanical emissions related to UAS. Although the different modalities seem quite different from one another, they adhere to relatively similar general emission-based principles. This section aims to provide a basic understanding of these general principles.

The basic principles used for detection and tracking are similar for different sensing modalities. As UAS generate emissions or alter emissions from external sources, natural external emitters, artificially created emissions or emissions of UAS can be used for establishing the presence of UAS.

UAS are regarded as a multi-faceted and multi-layered threat that is hard to address. Common understanding among experts is that there are significant improvements required before C‑UAS systems can provide a sufficient level of protection against the current - and rapidly evolving - threat. Due to the ever-evolving nature of the threat, C‑UAS will form a continuous cat and mouse game, and even if a “silver bullet” solution would materialise, it could become obsolete in several months (Muller et al., 2021). By addressing the technologies from the underlying principles, the reader should have solid starting point in understanding current and future developments in the technology.

evidence.section: 2.1 Simple example of human UAS detection and tracking
evidence.original_text: Many factors of different origins can influence the performance of the spotter in the above example. These spotter-performance influencing factors can be grouped in different categories, such as UA related factors, environmental factors and spotter related factors. Table 1 provides an overview of example factors that influence the performance of a human spotter. The table also provides an example indication of a lower bound (relatively easy situations) and an upper bound (relatively difficult situations) for these factors influencing the performance of a human spotter. Table 1 illustrates several factors that contribute to the performance of a spotter. Some have to do with the spotter, e.g. when spotting is the only task at hand, it is easier to spot a UA compared to when other tasks also must be attended. Some of the factors can be compensated for, often only to a certain extent, as e.g. the spotter might be able to compensate for nighttime darkness by using a flashlight to illuminate the UA. But many other factors cannot be influenced by the spotter. Figure 6 illustrates several of the many performance-influencing factors: A bigger UA (1) is easier to spot than a smaller one (2). A close by UA (1) is easier to spot than a faraway UA (3) A UA in dry weather (1) is easier to spot than a UA in heavy rain conditions (4) A UA in clear sight (1) is easier to spot than one (partially) behind a building (5)

evidence.section: 2.1 Simple example of human UAS detection and tracking
evidence.original_text: During real life C UAS operations it would be ideal if the underlying C UAS technologies function flawlessly under any condition, including in situations in which many influencing factors might be highly challenging. It is however important for the reader to realize that every C UAS system has its limitations. The following section focusses on the general principles behind detection and tracking techniques and illustrates some of the challenges.

In registering emissions, C‑UAS sensors have limitations. These limitations have to do with the coverage, the range and requirements relating to the propagation of the emissions of interest. The limitations are briefly touched upon below. Detection and tracking coverage shape and range: The directions and maximum range in which a C UAS sensor is capable of establishing the presence of UAS varies per sensor. This is an important aspect to consider when planning for a C‑UAS solution set-up. Two important qualities to take into account are the sensor’s coverage shape, and influencing factors for the maximum range. The coverage shape of a sensor is defined by characteristics of the sensor, like e.g. the camera specifications or the sensitivity in different directions. As an example, Figure 10 shows various coverage shapes that a sensor could have. The maximum range of a sensor is however not only defined by the characteristics of the sensor. Like the influencing factors for the performance of the human spotter in section 2.2, the environment and the characteristics of the UAS also influence the performance of the sensor. This is illustrated by the fact that heavy fog severely impacts the maximum detection range of a visual light based camera. It is also illustrated by the fact that the maximum detection range of a radar can differ per UA, as it depends on the radar visible size of a UA to detect. The radar visible size of a UA is referred to as the radar cross section (RCS) of the UA. The range of a sensor can often be calculated with physics-based models. The calculations can range from relatively simple models that require only a few parameters to more precise complex models that make use of many parameters. More complex models can take the environment and the characteristics of the UAS into account, resulting in different detection ranges for different environmental conditions and UAS. For practical reasons, the maximum range of a sensor might be specified as a fixed number, whereas it is important to realise that it might vary. The environment and characteristics of the UAS have an impact on the maximum range of a sensor.

Line‑of‑sight and electromagnetic propagation: Some sensors require line‑of‑sight (LoS) to be able to detect, localise and track UAS or UAS-related elements. LoS refers to the absence of obstructions between the sensor and the UAS or UAS-related element. The implication of LoS is that objects that block (parts of) the sensor coverage shape create blind spots for the sensor, so called occlusion areas. Figure 11 provides an example of the coverage of a sensor being limited by an obstacle. In areas with many objects, like urban environments, obstacles create many challenges when trying to optimize coverage for a C UAS system set-up. The 3D coverage map of LoS based sensors is relatively easy to determine, as it is relatively easy to determine their blind spots. Blind spots can be reduced or eliminated by adding sensors that (partially) cover the blind spot. The coverage area for some sensors is not necessarily determined by line‑of‑sight. These types of sensors function based on the propagation of emissions. They merely focus on the presence of an emission, without the need to receive the emission directly from the UAS. Radio frequency based sensors, for example, allow for emissions to be reflected and refracted by all sorts of objects before they reach the sensor, as long as the emission levels are still sufficiently clearly distinguishable from background noise. The propagation-based type of sensor allows for coverage of areas that not necessarily have line‑of‑sight and might allow for “looking around a corner”. Figure 12 provides an example of the propagation of RF signals not being limited to line of sight. Propagation-based sensors have a coverage area that is hard to determine, making it difficult to identify blind spots. Calculating the coverage area is complex, as it requires sophisticated propagation calculations that make use of detailed 3D models, including information on the materials at the building façade and their reflectiveness for the specific type of emissions. As an alternative to calculations, the coverage area can be sampled by running coverage tests at a 3D grid of test points.

RF detection depends on a library of “known” signals of UAS elements, which makes its proper functioning susceptible to firmware updates of the UAS elements: when the UAS is updated and e.g. uses a new communication waveform, the RF sensing system might not recognize the communications anymore as relating to a UAS. RF sensing will not sense UAS flying without RF communication, e.g. a UA autonomously flying from one waypoint to another. Performance and range of the system are significantly influenced by the general use of the frequencies the system operates on. In case of major events with many visitors making use of the EMS, e.g. by streaming video from their phones, this increases the workload for the system and negatively affects both the performance and range of the system. Packet inspection-based systems have some potential downsides, as an increasing rate of encryption of UAS communication might slowly but surely eliminate the possibility to inspect/interpret the RF information received. Packet inspection-based systems can also easily be spoofed with false RF messages, e.g. with fake locations of UA and operators. Spoofing can create an additional and unnecessary workload, as it could result in follow-up being sent to the spoofed locations.

The data fusion process is far from straightforward and introduces many challenges. One of the challenging aspects is that the data from the different sensing systems is likely to be based on different moments in time, arrive at different times, with different update frequencies and different uncertainties. The sensor data fusion algorithm has to figure out how to piece all the data together into an accurate, reliable, and comprehensive overview. Challenging example: To illustrate some of the challenges for the data fusion, a C UAS solution equipped with a radar, an RF sensing system and a combined visual light / IR camera, set-up around a large outdoor event, is used as an example. The radar and the RF sensing system are located close to each other, yet have different coverage areas. The coverage area of the radar is about 2 km for a small consumer UA, yet that of the RF sensing system is close to 3 km for the same type of UA. The camera is located close to both other sensing systems. Both the radar and the camera cannot see through/behind obstacles, yet due to multipath the RF sensing system’s coverage also includes some of the occluded areas. Blind C‑UAS tests are performed on the day before the event starts: unknown to the C‑UAS solution, different UA take off from various locations, to verify if the C UAS solution functions properly. During the test, the C UAS system appears to work fine: either the RF sensing system or both the RF and radar detect the UA within less 30 seconds after take-off of a UA and provide updates on the location within maximally 10 seconds in between. When both systems detect the UA, even though there are inherent uncertainties in their localisation of the UA, the sensor data fusion succeeds in matching the detections and provide a clear location of a single UA. The visual light / IR camera is used to confirm the detection and localisation of the UA. When the event starts, people start coming into the event area, and start to make use of the on-site Wi-Fi and mobile phone networks. As the frequency bands used for controlling UAS are the same as those used for Wi-Fi, the traffic on frequencies that are monitored by the C UAS RF sensing system increases significantly. This puts more and more load on the RF sensing system that tries to recognize all sorts of UAS related signals within the captured data. Due to interference the detection range for the RF sensing system starts to reduce. Then, in the evening of the event, after sundown, a UAS light show has been planned by the organisation. When the UAS of the light show start to take off, quite some people of the audience take out their mobile phone and start streaming. The C UAS system is expected to start detecting, localising and tracking the UAS of the light show shortly after take-off, yet the system turns out to require several minutes before reporting only some of the UAS, and provides infrequent updates of their locations, with sometimes more than a minute in between in which a UA potentially has travelled quite some distance. When looking into the data provided by the individual sensing systems, it turns out that the RF sensing system has a hard time detecting, localising and tracking the UAS due to the significant use of the frequency bands that it operates on. At the same time, the radar has difficulties in discriminating one UAS from another, and sometimes cannot see a UA, e.g. because another UA is flying in front of it. As a result, the data fusion algorithms get incomplete information from sometimes moments apart that are very difficult to fuse. The above example illustrates that data fusion can be of great help in consolidating the findings of different sensing systems, yet at the same time, there will also be situations in which data fusion will have a hard time in matching the data coming in from different sources.

Common understanding among experts is that there are significant improvements required before C‑UAS systems can provide a sufficient level of protection against the current - and rapidly evolving - threat. Due to the ever-evolving nature of the threat, C‑UAS will form a continuous cat and mouse game, and even if a “silver bullet” solution would materialise, it could become obsolete in several months (Muller et al., 2021). By addressing the technologies from the underlying principles, the reader should have solid starting point in understanding current and future developments in the technology.

In October 2023, the European Commission (EC) published a Communication on countering the potential threats from non cooperative unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) (European Commission, 2023). The communication responds to the need to: provide a comprehensive and harmonised policy framework; build a common understanding of applicable procedures to face the continuously evolving threats possibly posed by UAS; take into account the rapid developments of both the threat and C‑UAS technology. The Communication complements the European Union (EU) framework on UAS, and addresses UAS as a current and rapidly evolving threat. It indicates the relevance of keeping pace with technological developments. In formulating an EU counter drone policy, the Communication identifies key activity areas that are clustered into six pillars, as indicated in Figure 1. This report primarily contributes to the EC key activity of community building & information sharing, and facilitates activities related to C‑UAS systems testing, practical guidance & operational support and C‑UAS R&I in general. The target audience is the Commission C‑UAS expert group, the Member States and critical entities and authorities responsible for the protection of public spaces in Europe.

Counter Counter-UAS: There exist systems that try to prevent a C UAS system from proper functioning, the so-called counter C‑UAS systems. One approach taken by those systems is to generate fake emissions that are then detected by the sensing systems of the C UAS system, thereby further complicating the tasks of fusion. Yet at the same time, when properly set-up, sensing system fusion might prevent counter C‑UAS solutions from being successful with relatively simple measures, as the fusion might be able to quickly eliminate the fake emissions detected by one sensing system, as the accompanying emissions that should have been detected by another type of sensing system, might not be present where they should have been.
